does: This paper reformulates safety alignment in large language models as a per-category constrained optimization problem rather than treating safety as a single global objective. The authors derive Cat-DPO, an algorithm based on direct preference optimization that maintains separate adaptive safety margins for each harm category. Instead of applying uniform safety constraints across all preference pairs, the method tightens margins when a category exhibits unsafe behavior and relaxes them as performance improves, allowing the training signal to dynamically track each category's difficulty. Experiments across two LLM backbones and six preference-learning baselines show that Cat-DPO improves both aggregate helpfulness and harmlessness while reducing per-category safety variance and the performance gap between easiest and hardest harm categories.
connects: This work extends the direct preference optimization framework developed for general alignment by introducing category-awareness into safety training. It responds to the known limitation that single-scalar safety objectives cause models to perform unevenly across different harm types, with some categories remaining vulnerable despite average safety. The approach builds on constrained optimization methods in reinforcement learning from human feedback and preference learning, adapting techniques like adaptive margins or temperature scaling from other domains. The per-category framing draws on curriculum learning and adaptive weighting schemes that have been applied to imbalanced learning problems, bringing structured multi-task optimization perspectives to the safety alignment problem.
enables: This categorical approach opens pathways toward more nuanced and robust safety guarantees. Future work could develop hierarchical safety frameworks that group related harms or establish safety budgets per category for deployment contexts. The adaptive margin mechanism suggests possibilities for integrating real-world safety feedback to dynamically adjust training as new failure modes emerge in practice. The method enables safety researchers to diagnose and address long-tail harm categories more systematically rather than optimizing homogeneously. It also supports developing safety profiles customized to different application domains by weighting categories according to context-specific risks, and could facilitate better understanding of category-specific model behaviors through interpretability work focused on harm-specific learned representations.
